programs.name: Medicaid
programs.what_it_does: Medicaid provides comprehensive health coverage to low-income seniors, covering doctor visits, hospital stays, prescription medications, and long-term care. It significantly reduces out-of-pocket medical expenses for eligible individuals.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors and disabled individuals who meet Medicaid eligibility criteria.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by services; covers medical costs including long-term care.[2]

programs.name: Community Based Alternatives (CBA) Waiver
programs.what_it_does: The CBA Waiver pays for services associated with assisted living facilities and community-based alternatives to institutional care. It supports seniors transitioning to or remaining in community settings rather than nursing homes.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Medicaid-eligible seniors and disabled individuals meeting functional criteria.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Pays for personal care services (not room/board); value varies by assessed needs.[4]

programs.name: STAR+PLUS Managed Care Program
programs.what_it_does: Delivers comprehensive Medicaid-managed long-term care services including in-home support, nursing, and community-based care. It coordinates tailored services to help seniors stay in their homes or communities.
programs.who_its_for: Medicaid-eligible seniors and disabled individuals needing long-term services.
programs.whats_unique: Texas-specific Medicaid managed care model integrating acute and long-term care, emphasizing home and community-based alternatives to institutional care.
programs.benefit_value: Full spectrum of long-term care services; promotes aging in place.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.what_it_does: PACE provides comprehensive medical and social services to eligible seniors in community settings, including adult day health care and hospitalization when needed. It coordinates all Medicare and Medicaid benefits through a single provider.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 55+ certified as nursing facility eligible who can live safely in the community.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Full coverage of approved services at no cost to dual-eligible participants.[6]

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: These programs help pay Medicare Part A and B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments for qualifying low-income seniors. They are administered through Texas HHS as Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), and Qualifying Individual (QI).[2]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Medicare beneficiaries meeting specific income and resource limits.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Up to full coverage of Medicare costs depending on program (e.g., QMB covers most out-of-pocket).[2]

programs.name: Texas Simplified Application Project (TSAP) for SNAP
programs.what_it_does: TSAP simplifies the SNAP application process for seniors with a shorter form, no interview at renewal, and a 3-year certification period instead of the standard 6 months. It also sets a higher resource limit of $4,500 for seniors.
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 60+ who meet SNAP income guidelines.
programs.whats_unique: Unique simplified SNAP process exclusive to Texas seniors, with extended certification and waived renewal interviews not standard federally.
programs.benefit_value: Food benefits based on income; simplified access saves time and reduces barriers.

programs.name: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.what_it_does: LIHEAP helps eligible low-income households meet home heating and cooling needs through cash grants or direct vendor payments. It prevents utility shutoffs and supports energy cost management.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors, including those 60+ prioritized in Texas.[6]
programs.benefit_value: One-time grants averaging $200-$500 depending on household size and needs.[6]

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.what_it_does: WAP funds energy efficiency upgrades like insulation, sealing, and furnace repairs to reduce energy costs. It improves home safety and comfort for low-income households.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors and disabled individuals; priority for 60+.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Free home improvements valued at $5,000-$7,000 on average.[6]

programs.name: Texas State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.what_it_does: SHIP offers free one-on-one counseling and assistance with Medicare questions, plan choices, and appeals. It is provided through Area Agencies on Aging (AAAs).[5]
programs.who_its_for: Texas residents with Medicare, especially aged 60+.[5]
programs.benefit_value: Free counseling services; no financial eligibility required.[5]

programs.name: Meals on Wheels (via Area Agencies on Aging)
programs.what_it_does: Provides home-delivered nutritious meals to homebound seniors to promote health and independence. Often coordinated through AAAs and local providers.[1][5]
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors aged 60+ who are unable to prepare meals.[5]
programs.benefit_value: Meals at low or no cost (e.g., $3-$5/suggested donation); 5 meals/week typically.[5]

programs.name: Family Caregiver Support Program
programs.what_it_does: Offers caregiver training, respite care, and support services to help family caregivers assist seniors at home. Funded through AAAs and HHS.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of seniors 60+ or disabled adults 18+.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Respite hours, training, and counseling; value varies by assessment.[3]

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides part-time paid community service work training and employment opportunities for low-income seniors. Helps transition to unsubsidized employment.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage pay at 20 hours/week; job placement support.[6]

programs.name: Legal Aid for Seniors (via AAAs)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free legal assistance and representation for issues like benefits, housing, and abuse prevention. Offered through Area Agencies on Aging networks.[5]
programs.benefit_value: Free legal services; no charge regardless of income for prioritization.[5]

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in nursing homes and assisted living facilities, resolving complaints and protecting rights. Operated through AAAs statewide.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Residents of long-term care facilities and their families.[5]
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy and complaint resolution services.[5]

programs.name: Texas Tax Deferral for Seniors
programs.what_it_does: Allows eligible seniors to defer property taxes on their homestead until it is sold or transferred. Seniors can also opt for up to a 20% local exemption through county appraisal districts.
programs.who_its_for: Homeowners aged 65+ meeting income and residency requirements.
programs.whats_unique: State-specific tax deferral option combined with optional local exemptions, providing flexible property tax relief beyond federal programs.
programs.benefit_value: Defers full property taxes; up to 20% exemption reduces taxable value.

programs.name: Medically Needy Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides Medicaid coverage to seniors who exceed regular income limits but have high medical expenses that reduce their income below the threshold. It covers healthcare costs after 'spending down' excess income on medical bills.
programs.who_its_for: Seniors with income above Medicaid limits but high medical costs, often on fixed incomes.
programs.whats_unique: Texas state-funded Medicaid 'spend-down' program not available in all states, acting as a safety net for those with fluctuating medical expenses.
programs.benefit_value: Medicaid health coverage after spend-down; significant relief for high medical bills.

programs.name: In-Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS)
programs.what_it_does: Provides assistance with activities of daily living such as bathing, dressing, meals, and medication management to support independent living. Offered through local agencies like Area Agencies on Aging to avoid nursing home placement.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors aged 60+ needing help with daily activities, often waitlisted based on priority.
programs.whats_unique: State-funded expansion of HCBS through Texas HHS and local ADRCs, with specific implementation like Dallas Area Agency on Aging, beyond federal minimums.
programs.benefit_value: Personal care hours weekly/monthly; enables home-based living, saving on facility costs.

programs.name: Texas Aging and Disability Resource Centers (ADRCs)
programs.what_it_does: Serves as a 'No Wrong Door' single entry point offering personalized guidance, referrals for in-home care, housing, transportation, respite, and eligibility help across all 254 counties. Simplifies navigation of state and federal long-term services.
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 60+, people with disabilities, veterans, and caregivers seeking long-term support.
programs.whats_unique: Statewide network unique to Texas covering every county with integrated 'No Wrong Door' system for streamlined access not uniformly replicated elsewhere.
programs.benefit_value: Free counseling and referrals; connects to services worth thousands in annual support.
